text: This is a fifty-nine-year-old active male with an incarcerated left-sided direct inguinal hernia. He was seen in the office. Had symptoms only for a few weeks.
You can see working lateral to medial as always, no change here. The medial umbilical ligaments seem to be quite different in every population for whatever reason.

The surgeon creates a preperitoneal flap by dissecting the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneum, which converge at the inferior epigastric vessels. The surgeon uses electrocautery to push the fat laterally, taking down the flap in a lateral direction.

The surgeon encounters crossing vessels near the inferior epigastric vessels and uses cautery to gently push the antisac away, taking care to avoid the iliohypogastric nerve located medially above the muscle layer.

The surgeon dissects down to the pubic tubercle and Cooper's ligament, staying close to the left iliac vein. The dissection reveals minimal extra fat, with the surgeon maintaining proximity to the pubic bone.
The surgeon pulls the hernia sac down at an oblique angle beyond the symphysis pubis on the contralateral side, creating a small tear in the peritoneal flap.

The surgeon identifies the vas deferens as it comes into view, noting the proximity to the iliac vein. Careful manipulation is required to avoid buckling the mesh, as the surgeon navigates below the point where the mesh begins to fall underneath the iliac vein.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for the presence of a cord lipoma, ensuring careful adherence to established procedural guidelines.

The surgeon positions the mesh with precision, navigating between the coronamortis vein and the iliac vein. The triangular fixation technique is applied, with high medial and high lateral fixation points visible.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap in a standard fashion, aligning the medial umbilical ligament and the preperitoneal fat plane.
